items.title: Shocking DNA Study Reveals The Plague Is Prehistoric
items.description:
Yersinia Pestis - the most feared disease in human history - has long been thought to be the Plague of Justinian in 541 AD. But new studies of ancient DNA have revealed traces of Yersinia pestis dating back more than 5000 years. 

In this episode of The Ancients, Tristan Hughes is joined by Laura Spinney to explore the origins of prehistoric plague. How did this disease emerge in the Neolithic world? Where did it come from? And could it have triggered a Stone Age collapse which signalled the daw

items.title: "The King Himself Hath Eat of Them" | A Biscuit Recipe for Charles II
items.description:
In 1660 the English crown was restored and Charles II began his reign as the 'Merry Monarch'. We might think he would have a taste for decadent foods, but actually, simple small cakes were 'much esteemed' at the Restoration court.

In this video, our Historic Kitchens Team follow 17th Century cook Mrs Stockdel's recipe, figuring out the best method from the simple instructions, to see how 'excellent' they really are. Are these cakes (or biscuits) fit for a king?

Read the original recipe for the

items.title: AI Writing is Taking Over
items.description:
Watch Amy's Dead-End Dreamhouse on Nebula and save 40% on an annual subscription: https://go.nebula.tv/dreamhouse?/ref=jaredhenderson

Or check out my latest video: https://go.nebula.tv/jaredhenderson

Fake AI books are flooding platforms like Amazon. But that's not the only kind of AI writing that is affecting us. AI is now being used to write police reports and legal briefs. 

Read my newsletter: https://jaredhenderson.substack.com

SOURCES
https://cops.usdoj.gov/html/dispatch/01-2025/ai_repor

global_silver_linings.title: 4 Beluga Whales from Shuttered Canadian Park Arrive at Chicago’s Shedd Aquarium After Rescue (LOOK)
global_silver_linings.snippet: When GNN reported last week that Canadian and US authorities approved the emergency transfer of some 30 beluga whales from a closed theme park in Ontario, little had been released about how one goes about moving a several ton marine mammal across state boundaries. Then, news broke that 4 of those 30 whales had already […] The post 4 Beluga Whales from Shuttered Canadian Park Arrive at Chicago’s Shedd Aquarium After Rescue (LOOK) appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: Four beluga whales that faced an uncertain future when their Canadian park closed have found a safe, professional home at Shedd Aquarium — a reminder that when institutions work together, vulnerable animals don't get left behind. It's a real second chance, hard-won and well-deserved.

global_silver_linings.title: What went right this week: the good news that matters
global_silver_linings.snippet: The world’s road got much safer, global hunger levels eased, and Brazil set a welcome record for deforestation, plus more The post What went right this week: the good news that matters appeared first on Positive News .
global_silver_linings.reason: In a media landscape that can feel relentlessly heavy, a dedicated roundup of genuinely good news is a quiet act of service — proof that positive things are happening every week, even when they don't make the loudest headlines.

global_silver_linings.title: 11-year-old boy shows his American pride by crocheting 8-foot flag using 60 skeins of yarn
global_silver_linings.snippet: Greyson Moore learned to crochet by watching YouTube tutorials. The post 11-year-old boy shows his American pride by crocheting 8-foot flag using 60 skeins of yarn appeared first on Upworthy .
global_silver_linings.reason: An 11-year-old turning 60 skeins of yarn into an eight-foot crocheted flag is a lovely reminder that creativity, patience, and quiet dedication are alive and well in the next generation.

global_silver_linings.title: From 300 to 3,000: how Australia brought the numbat back
global_silver_linings.snippet: BY THE OPTIMIST DAILY EDITORIAL TEAM Great news! The (adorable) numbat is no longer endangered. The IUCN has moved Western Australia’s animal emblem from Endangered to Near Threatened on its Red List, after more than 40 years of conservation work.
global_silver_linings.reason: Australia's numbat population has grown tenfold — from just 300 to 3,000 — showing that targeted, committed conservation work can genuinely pull a species back from the edge. This small, striking marsupial is living proof that extinction is not always inevitable.

discovery_articles.title: Medieval Strip Farming in England
discovery_articles.snippet: The medieval practice of strip forming is similar to the "strip cropping" practices carried out in some midwestern states of the USA, except that the origins come from a need to equitably divide land resources around a settlement (strictly managed by the lord of the manor and his officials) rather than having the specific aim of soil conservation and crop management which seems to drive strip cropping in the USA. Medieval strip farming did achieve such aims but it was secondary to the resource allocation issue during the feudal medieval period. In England, the evidence of the strip farming (also called open field farming) having taken place over long periods of time, in the past, is very common.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: Those faint ridge-and-furrow undulations still visible in English fields aren't just geography — they're the lasting impression of a feudal fairness system, land carved into strips so no single peasant got all the good soil. The earth itself remembers the medieval argument over who gets which patch.

discovery_articles.title: Gates of Heaven Synagogue in Madison, Wisconsin
discovery_articles.snippet: Built in 1863 by a small group of German-speaking Jewish families, this Romanesque Revival structure has served many roles over the decades, including a sanctuary, a dentist's office, and a campaign headquarters. When threatened with demolition in 1971, the community rallied to save it, famously rolling the 200-ton building through the streets to its current lakefront home in James Madison Park, where it now serves as a cherished public venue for weddings and community events. The synagogue was originally commissioned by a small congregation of 17 German-speaking Jewish families.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: In 1971, Madisonians literally rolled a 200-ton synagogue through city streets rather than let it fall to a wrecking ball — which may be the most charmingly stubborn act of historic preservation on record. It now hosts weddings on a lakefront, having survived demolition, dentistry, and political campaigning along the way.

discovery_articles.title: Experimental Alzheimer's Drug That Fights a Tough-to-Target Protein Shows Promise at Slowing Cognitive Decline in a Clinical Trial
discovery_articles.snippet: Recently FDA-approved treatments work to clear clumps of the protein amyloid beta in the brain. A drug that instead goes after tangles of the protein tau can yield similar cognitive outcomes, a new study suggests
discovery_articles.ferns_note: For decades, Alzheimer's research placed its biggest bet on clearing amyloid plaques — but tau tangles, the other villain lurking in the diseased brain, may be just as worthy a target. The quiet implication here is that we may have been fighting only half the war.

discovery_articles.title: Months After Burglars Stole Crown Jewels From the Louvre, the Apollo Gallery Reopens, Highlighting Its Origins as a Royal Reception Hall
discovery_articles.snippet: Museum officials hope visitors will appreciate the gilded hall, conceived by Louis XIV more than 360 years ago, for its history and ornamentation, even without the crown jewels
discovery_articles.ferns_note: The Apollo Gallery was designed by Louis XIV as a stage for royal magnificence, so it's almost poetically fitting that its crown jewels were stolen and the hall still managed to upstage the loss. There's something very Sun King about a room that refuses to be outshone.

discovery_articles.title: Wacky, Mysterious Paintings by This Surrealist Still Influence Contemporary Artists. An Exhibition in London Will Explore the Legacy of René Magritte
discovery_articles.snippet: The Belgian Surrealist was famous for his playful subversion, like when he created a painting of a pipe and then defiantly declared it wasn’t a pipe, after all
discovery_articles.ferns_note: Magritte's pipe wasn't mischief for mischief's sake — it was a quiet philosophical grenade lobbed at the idea that images and reality are the same thing, a question that feels more urgent now than ever. Nearly a century later, we're still staring at screens wondering what, exactly, is a pipe.

discovery_articles.title: Different moon rocks tell different tales about a long-ago bombardment
discovery_articles.snippet: Prior moon rock samples, including from Apollo, seem heavily influenced by one impact. The farside samples tweak the story.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: The Apollo mission rocks told us one version of the moon's violent past — but it turns out they may have been narrating a single catastrophic event rather than the whole story. Samples from the far side are gently rewriting the bombardment timeline we thought we knew.

discovery_articles.title: The golden age of language was a few thousand years ago
discovery_articles.snippet: A few millennia ago, people worldwide may have spoken or signed up to 10 times as many languages as they do today.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: The world once held perhaps ten times as many languages as it does today — meaning the vast majority of human ways of thinking, naming, and dreaming have already gone silent. Language extinction isn't a modern crisis; it's an ancient, ongoing erasure.

discovery_articles.title: Sometimes Being First Means Seeing the End Before Anyone Else
discovery_articles.snippet: Determination and effort propelled Jacob Tsimerman to the top of the math world. But now that he’s won a Fields Medal, he worries that the field is in jeopardy. The post Sometimes Being First Means Seeing the End Before Anyone Else first appeared on Quanta Magazine
discovery_articles.ferns_note: Jacob Tsimerman reached the summit of mathematics young enough to see clearly what most prefer not to — that the field may be approaching problems too hard for unaided human minds. A Fields Medal and an existential worry, arriving together.

discovery_articles.title: Playful Knitted Pillows by Donna Wilson Infuse Interiors with Whimsy and Joy
discovery_articles.snippet: What's not to love about a pillow with personality? Do stories and artists like this matter to you? Become a Colossal Member today and support independent arts publishing for as little as $7 per month.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: Donna Wilson's knitted pillows are the rare decorative object that seems to have an inner life — somewhere between folk art and a friendly hallucination. Small, handmade, and quietly subversive of the idea that interiors should take themselves seriously.

featured_read.title: How to Keep Life from Becoming a Parody of Itself: Simone de Beauvoir on the Art of Growing Older
featured_read.snippet: "In old age we should wish still to have passions strong enough to prevent us turning in on ourselves."
featured_read.blurb: De Beauvoir's argument is quietly alarming: the real danger of aging isn't decline, but the slow contraction into a self that no longer reaches outward. This is the essay to read before you catch yourself saying you're too old for something.

garden_note.note: First light arrives over Zürich at 05:17 this morning, catching the lavender in that particular silvery way that makes the whole garden smell twice as strong — step outside before the heat builds and you'll have it almost to yourself. High summer is pressing down on everything now, so water deeply at the roots rather than the leaves, and let the soil tell you when it's had enough.
in_season: Courgettes — harvest before they become marrows
Deadhead dahlias to keep them flowering
Runner beans climbing and ready to pick
garden_note.sky_tonight: The waxing gibbous moon rises in the south-east by mid-evening, bright enough to cast soft shadows — look for Jupiter nearby in Taurus as darkness settles after 21:47.
garden_note.sun_times: First light at 05:17, sunrise at 05:52, sunset at 21:12, with dusk lingering until 21:47.

news.title: The 35 Best Kitchen Deals to Shop This Week from Le Creuset, Nordstrom, and More
news.snippet: Plus, score some exclusive discounts! READ MORE...
news.blurb: If your kitchen wishlist is longer than your grocery list, this round-up of Le Creuset and Nordstrom deals is a very good reason to treat yourself guilt-free.

news.title: Helen Goh’s recipe for pineapple and mango paletas | The sweet spot
news.snippet: These bright, sharp Mexican iced treats are the perfect summer cooler – with a spiky kick The first time I encountered paletas in Mexico, they felt strangely familiar. They reminded me of the iced treats of my Malaysian childhood: fruit-driven, brightly coloured and made less to indulge than to relieve the heat. What struck me most was the flavour logic shared by both countries, where sweetness is often enlivened with salt, chilli, tamarind or citrus, each sharpening the fruit in its own way and making it seem brighter, cooler and somehow more vivid.
news.blurb: Paletas — Mexico's gloriously unfussy answer to the ice lolly — are having a well-deserved moment, and this piece makes a compelling case for why your freezer should meet them immediately.

news.title: Squid Ink Spaghetti
news.snippet: Photo: Scott Semler • Food Styling: Camille Becerra Slow-cooked seafood forms a glossy black sauce for this pasta finished with herbs and a squeeze of lemon. The post Squid Ink Spaghetti appeared first on Saveur .
news.blurb: Slow-cooked seafood, a glossy jet-black sauce, and a bright hit of lemon: squid ink spaghetti is the kind of dramatic, deeply satisfying pasta that makes a Tuesday feel like an occasion.

recipe.title: Bean and Lentil Soup with Kale
recipe.snippet: A hearty tomato-based soup made with beans, lentils, kale, and a deep mushroom broth. More importantly, it freezes well so you always have a good meal at the ready. Continue reading Bean and Lentil Soup with Kale on 101 Cookbooks
recipe.blurb: This tomato-based bean and lentil soup with kale and mushroom broth is exactly the kind of quietly brilliant, deeply comforting pot you want simmering on the stove — and the fact that it freezes beautifully means future-you will be grateful too.

larder.seasonal_note: Zürich's Tuesday and Saturday markets at Bürkliplatz are flush with Zürcher Beerenzeit right now — the local strawberries have peaked and the first raspberries and golden mirabelles are jostling for space beside the stalls, begging to be folded into a simple Vermicelles or eaten straight with a spoonful of Halbrahm before the morning heat arrives.

fern_data.greeting: Somewhere right now, a gardener is already on their knees in the dirt before the heat wins — which feels like the right spirit for a digest that moves from prehistoric plague DNA to a 17th-century biscuit made for a king, with Simone de Beauvoir waiting patiently at the end.
